questions.question_text: Before filing a claim with the Georgia Real Estate Recovery Fund, what must the claimant first obtain?
questions.correct_answer: A civil court judgment against the licensee
distractors: A GREC disciplinary order
A police report documenting the loss
Written approval from the licensee's broker
questions.explanation: Claimants must first obtain a civil court judgment and demonstrate inability to collect before accessing the Recovery Fund. The Fund is a last resort after normal collection methods have been exhausted.

questions.question_text: A buyer obtains a judgment against a licensee in June 2025. By when must they file a Recovery Fund claim?
questions.correct_answer: Within one year of the final judgment
distractors: Within 90 days of the final judgment
Within two years of the transaction
Within 30 days of the GREC hearing
questions.explanation: Georgia requires Recovery Fund claims to be filed within one year of the final judgment. Missing this deadline bars the claimant from recovery regardless of the merits of their claim.

questions.question_text: A consumer suffers financial harm from a licensed agent acting in a personal capacity, not as an agent. Can they claim from the Recovery Fund?
questions.correct_answer: No, the licensee must have acted in licensed capacity
distractors: Yes, any financial harm by a licensee qualifies
Yes, if the total harm exceeds $5,000
Only if the licensee's sponsoring broker authorizes it
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund only covers losses caused by licensees acting in their licensed capacity. Personal business dealings, even if conducted by a licensee, fall outside the Fund's scope of coverage.

questions.question_text: How is the Georgia Real Estate Recovery Fund primarily funded?
questions.correct_answer: A portion of license and renewal fees
distractors: Annual state legislative appropriation
Fines collected from disciplinary actions
Voluntary contributions from brokerages
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund is sustained by a portion of license and renewal fees paid by all Georgia real estate licensees. This self-funding mechanism ensures the Fund remains available without relying on general tax revenue.

questions.question_text: A claimant with a valid judgment has assets they could seize from the licensee but prefers the Recovery Fund. Will the claim succeed?
questions.correct_answer: No, they must demonstrate inability to collect first
distractors: Yes, the Fund pays regardless of other assets
Yes, if the judgment exceeds $10,000
Only if the licensee consents in writing
questions.explanation: Claimants must demonstrate inability to collect the judgment through normal means before the Recovery Fund will pay. The Fund serves as a last resort, not a convenient alternative to standard collection efforts.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum Recovery Fund payment per individual claimant in Georgia?
distractors: $25,000 per claimant
$10,000 per claimant
$45,000 per claimant
questions.explanation: Georgia caps Recovery Fund payments at $15,000 per individual claimant. Even if a judgment exceeds this amount, the claimant can only recover up to $15,000 from the Fund.

questions.question_text: Two buyers each file valid Recovery Fund claims from the same fraudulent transaction by one licensee. What is the maximum combined payout?
distractors: $30,000 combined for both claimants
$15,000 total shared between them
$45,000 combined for both claimants
questions.explanation: The per-transaction cap is $25,000 regardless of how many claimants are involved. While each individual claimant is limited to $15,000, the total for one transaction cannot exceed $25,000.

questions.question_text: A licensee has already caused $40,000 in Recovery Fund payouts over several transactions. A new valid claim for $10,000 is filed. How much can be paid?
distractors: $10,000, the full claim amount
Nothing, the licensee's Fund is exhausted
$15,000, the per-claimant maximum
questions.explanation: Georgia imposes a $45,000 aggregate lifetime cap per licensee. With $40,000 already paid, only $5,000 remains available regardless of the individual claim amount or per-claimant limit.

questions.question_text: What is the aggregate lifetime maximum the Recovery Fund will pay for claims against any single Georgia licensee?
distractors: $25,000 per licensee lifetime
$75,000 per licensee lifetime
$100,000 per licensee lifetime
questions.explanation: Georgia limits total Recovery Fund exposure to $45,000 per licensee over their entire career. Once this cap is reached, no further Fund payments will be made for claims against that licensee.

questions.question_text: A claimant has a $20,000 judgment from a single transaction. What is the maximum they can recover from the Fund?
distractors: $20,000, the full judgment amount
$25,000, the per-transaction limit
$10,000, half the judgment
questions.explanation: Even though the judgment is $20,000 and the per-transaction limit is $25,000, the per-claimant cap of $15,000 applies. The claimant would need to pursue the remaining $5,000 through other means.

questions.question_text: What happens to a licensee's license when the Recovery Fund pays a claim against them?
questions.correct_answer: The license is automatically revoked by law
distractors: The license is placed on probation
The license is suspended for one year
Nothing, the Fund absorbs the loss
questions.explanation: When the Recovery Fund pays a claim, the licensee's license is automatically revoked by operation of law. This is mandatory and not subject to GREC discretion, reflecting the seriousness of causing Fund payouts.

questions.question_text: A licensee whose license was revoked due to a Recovery Fund payout wants reinstatement. What must they do?
questions.correct_answer: Repay the Fund in full plus interest
distractors: Complete 75 hours of additional education
Wait five years and reapply
Obtain written consent from the claimant
questions.explanation: Reinstatement requires full repayment to the Recovery Fund including interest. Until the licensee repays every dollar plus accrued interest, they cannot have their license reinstated under any circumstances.

questions.question_text: A broker argues that GREC should use discretion to avoid revoking their license after a Fund payout. Will this argument succeed?
questions.correct_answer: No, revocation is automatic and not discretionary
distractors: Yes, if the broker shows good faith
Yes, if the payout was under $5,000
Only if the broker agrees to probation
questions.explanation: License revocation upon Recovery Fund payment operates by law, not by GREC discretion. No mitigating factors or arguments can prevent revocation once the Fund pays a valid claim against the licensee.

questions.question_text: After repaying the Recovery Fund in full with interest, what additional step must the former licensee take?
questions.correct_answer: Apply for reinstatement of their license
distractors: Nothing further; the license is automatically restored
Pass the state licensing exam again only
Serve a mandatory two-year waiting period
questions.explanation: Repayment is necessary but not automatically sufficient. The former licensee must apply for reinstatement after repaying the Fund. Repayment makes reinstatement possible but does not guarantee it.

questions.question_text: The Recovery Fund pays $15,000 for a claim against Broker Smith. Six months later, Smith repays $10,000 and requests partial reinstatement. What is the result?
questions.correct_answer: Denied; full repayment plus interest is required
distractors: Granted with practice restrictions
Granted on probation until balance is paid
Granted if Smith's broker vouches for them
questions.explanation: Georgia requires full repayment of the Fund payout plus interest before reinstatement is possible. Partial payments, regardless of amount, do not qualify for any form of reinstatement or conditional licensing.
